markdown.markdown:
- Sections begin this week.
- Office hours start this week. See the Canvas discussion item for times/locations.
- Problem Set 1 is posted; it is due next Tuesday.
- The first weekly quiz will happen tomorrow:
  - Five multiple choice questions
  - 10 minutes
  - Anytime between 12:00am–11:59pm
  - Open-book and open-note, but **not** “open-friend” or “open-AI”

## Class Outline

- What is a monopoly?
- Monopoly profit maximization
- Welfare under monopoly

## Recap from last class

- The perfectly competitive market maximizes welfare.
- There is no deadweight loss and, in the long run, firms produce at the minimum of their average cost curves.
- Three common sources of market failure:
  - Firms are not price-takers (e.g., monopoly)
  - Actions create benefits or impose costs on others (e.g., air pollution)
  - Some parties have information that others don’t (e.g., used car reliability)

We begin exploring the first today.

# I. What is a monopoly?

## Definition

A **monopoly** is a sole supplier of a good with no close substitute.

Examples:
- Historical: Hudson Bay Company; British East India Company; U.S. Steel; Aluminum Company of America (ALCOA)
- Modern: many utilities (e.g., PG&E); firms with patents (e.g., Pfizer)

## Assumptions of monopoly

- There is a single firm.
- The firm sets a single price for all customers.
- Barriers to entry exist.

Implications:
- A monopoly is **not** a price-taker: its production decision affects market price.
- With barriers to entry, a monopoly can earn economic profits in the long run.

## iClicker question

Are monopolies good or bad from a welfare perspective?

- Good
- Bad
- It depends
- I don’t know

(Section links: 100B-A: https://join.iclicker.com/HTCC; 100B-B: https://join.iclicker.com/MZWB)

## How can a monopoly arise? (Perloff 11.4)

[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: slide figure on sources of monopoly power / barriers to entry]

# II. Monopoly profit maximization (Perloff 11.1)

## Marginal revenue under perfect competition

Every unit a perfectly competitive firm sells can be sold at the market price  
$\Rightarrow$ the MR curve is horizontal.

[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: TR(q) / MR depiction for perfect competition]

## Perfectly competitive firm vs monopoly (MR intuition)

[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: side-by-side comparison of PC firm vs monopoly (P–q) with demand/MR]

## Profit maximization for monopoly: why MR differs from price

Higher production affects monopoly revenue in two ways:

- Revenue rises because the firm sells more units (**Region A**).
- But the firm must cut price to sell more; it loses revenue on inframarginal units (**Region B**).

[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: monopoly demand diagram with Regions A and B labeled]

## Numerical example: Regions A and B

A numerical example clearly shows the two regions A and B.

[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: numerical example table/steps (A and B) — part 1]  
[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: numerical example table/steps (A and B) — part 2]  
[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: numerical example table/steps (A and B) — part 3]  
[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: numerical example table/steps (A and B) — part 4]  
[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: numerical example table/steps (A and B) — part 5]  
[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: numerical example table/steps (A and B) — part 6]  
[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: numerical example table/steps (A and B) — part 7]

## What can we learn from the monopolist MR equation?

[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: slide with MR equation and interpretation]

## Using MR to solve the monopoly problem

Now, let’s use our knowledge of MR to solve the monopoly profit-maximization problem.

## Solving the monopoly problem

[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: worked example setup (demand/cost, MR/MC)]

## iClicker question

What value of $q$ will maximize the monopolist’s profits?

- $Q=80$
- $Q=60$
- $Q=40$
- $Q=20$
- Something else / I don’t know

(Section links: 100B-A: https://join.iclicker.com/HTCC; 100B-B: https://join.iclicker.com/MZWB)

## Solving the monopoly problem (continued)

[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: worked example solution steps — part 1]  
[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: worked example solution steps — part 2]

## What price will the monopolist set, and does the monopoly earn profits?

[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: price choice and profit calculation / diagram]

## Graphical depiction of a monopoly

[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: monopoly diagram with D, MR, MC and labeled quantities/prices (e.g., P=60, Q=40 etc.)]

## One slick trick to keep in mind…

[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: “slick trick” slide (likely geometry/markup rule)]

# III. Monopoly and welfare (Perloff 11.2)

## iClicker question: surplus comparison

Will consumer and producer surplus be different under a monopoly than perfect competition?

- No. They will be the same.
- Yes. CS will be higher and PS will be lower.
- Yes. CS will be lower and PS will be higher.
- Yes. Both CS and PS will be lower.
- I don’t know.

## iClicker question: welfare comparison

Will welfare be different under a monopoly than perfect competition?

- Yes. It will be higher.
- No. It will be the same.
- Yes. It will be lower.
- It depends.
- I don’t know.

## Revisit the example from last class

[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: competitive market CS/PS diagram (Q=300, P=6, etc.)]

## What would happen in a monopoly?

[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: monopoly version of the market diagram]

## Monopoly and welfare

[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: monopoly welfare diagram with CS, PS, DWL]

## Interpretation

The monopoly has two effects on welfare:

- It creates a deadweight loss (DWL) triangle since demand is greater than MC for $Q$ between 200 and 300.
- It captures surplus from consumers (A) with higher valuations (a transfer from consumers to producers).

[IMAGE PLACEHOLDER: welfare diagram highlighting DWL and transfer region A]

## Major takeaways

- Monopolies arise for a variety of reasons; some are good for society and some are bad.
- Like perfectly competitive firms, monopolies set $MR = MC$ to maximize profits.
- But monopolies are not price-takers:
  - They face a downward-sloping demand curve and a downward-sloping MR curve.
- A profit-maximizing monopoly cuts output below the competitive level and raises price.
- Cutting output below the competitive level creates a DWL triangle.
- It also transfers surplus from consumers to producers.

## Action Items

- Weekly quiz is tomorrow (12:00am–11:59pm). For the full 10 minutes, start by 11:49pm.
- Problem Set 1 is posted:
  - Canvas → Files → Problem Sets
  - Due next Tuesday at the start of class
  - Submit on Canvas
- Textbook is available under “Bookshelf” on Canvas
- Next class: Perloff 11.3–11.5
